Okay, welcome back. So before we go any deeper into this program, I really want to take a second to dispel any anxiety you may still have around writing long form sales copy. More specifically, I want you to get used to the idea that it's not this intimidating esoteric thing that will take decades to master after you read all these books that were written in the 50s back when doctors endorsed cigarettes and sexist ads were all the rage. So instead, I just really, really want to initiate you into a more intuitive and liveening approach that I call coaching the conversion. And what it all really comes down to is this one simple premise. It's the premise that the version of your prospect who buys your product is fundamentally different than the person who entered your funnel, or in this case, started reading your sales page. Instead, they've undergone a series of belief shifts, micro commitments and these transformations to make the journey from point A to point buyer. And it's your job as the conversion coach to identify and coach those shifts and transformations across the various sections of your sales page. You know, no one wakes up with their credit card in hand excited to spend hundreds or thousands of dollars. To do that, it requires a shift in belief in attitude and understanding and even identity level shifts to break through that inertia, catalyze that transformation between prospect and buyer. And as a coach and educator, a trainer, why would you wait until money changes hands before leading that transformation? You know, that work starts the second you come, the second they come into your sphere of influence, which in this case is your sales page and gives you that opportunity to coach them from a pre customer into a customer. And that has always been the key to double digit conversions. Sales specialists and coaches who enroll clients regularly understand this and that's why those conversations can close at 20, 40 or even 50% or beyond. And what we're doing here is we're taking those same dynamics and trying to retain as much intimacy and responsiveness as possible and coaching that conversion at scale on something like a sales page. And when you realize that the typical sales page closes at 1 to 3% and a good enrollment specialist closes at 20 to 50%, you realize that there's an amazing opportunity to bridge that 10X gap at scale. And that's exactly what we're going for here. So whereas 10X launches shows you how to coach that conversion and bridge that 10X gap across the different phases of an email launch sequence, 10X sales pages continues that theme and shows you how to do it vertically across a single asset. And we do that through what many copywriters will call message hierarchy. Now message hierarchy is really simply the sequence of transformation. You know, if we understand that the person who starts reading your sales page is fundamentally different than the person who buys your product, then it's the job of your sales page to coach that transformation in the right order. And each of the 15 points you'll see in the coming modules are designed to coach the right elements in the right sequence to create the highest probability of allowing that transformation to occur. And when you coach those elements in the right order, what you're doing is you're gradually building up your prospects ability to respond, which at the end of the day is the purest form or definition of direct response copywriting. But before we get there, I just want to wrap up with a few unexpected reasons why you need to have a sales page. So the first big reason to have a sales page is safety and perceived value. You know, on one level, it shows a certain form of sophistication and seriousness around your offer that you've invested the time and energy to create this asset that can act as that conversion coach. And that alone will build a lot of trust in your prospect. The second reason to have a sales page, and this gets a little more on the technical side, is the ability to pixel or retarget. So if you are planning to, or if you currently are running retargeting ads, then the sales page will be a really important piece of that puzzle to pixel as it signals a certain level of interest. So that's something you really want to keep in mind. The reason to create a sales page is just having that opportunity to pixel it and retarget those prospects later. The third one is something a little more recent, but it's the ability to incorporate live chat. In essence, coaching the conversion in real time. Having a sales page with a chat allows you to dynamically respond to objections and answer questions on the spot. But not only that, it's also a great tool for marketing research. I've always found that the best data collection comes in the heat of the moment in a real exchange in a sales context, not just in a pure research context. So the coach mining process you'll learn in the next module shows you how to do that on a sales call. But the chat box on a sales page is another opportunity to really mine some valuable insight. And finally, perhaps the most important reason to have a sales page is investment. You know, people who spend time spend money. You know, if they're reading, they're becoming more invested with every minute they spend reading your copy. It enhances their level of commitment and emotional investment, which are two very important factors in coaching the conversion. And a final note, everyone asks us how long should your sales page be? And people usually give the answer as long as it needs to be, which is very true, but also not very helpful. So as a rule of thumb, and this is just that a rule of thumb, mine typically come in at around 5000 words. But what it really comes down to is this. It comes down to understanding your prospects starting point and assessing how much hand holding and coaching they need to feel empowered to make a decision. The bigger decision, the more at stake, whether they buy or don't buy, then the more coaching they'll need to become the person capable and empowered enough to buy. And without you there to guide them in a one on one way, your sales page needs to do all of that heavy lifting. So how much, so how do we know how much heavy lifting is needed? Well, I like to use the 100 words per minute rule. For example, if it would take 60 seconds to coach the conversion, 100 words should be enough. So in the case of Apple earbuds, you could sell that in 60 seconds. Typically, you'll see about 100 words in product copy. For a health supplement, that might take you 15 minutes in a one on one conversation to empower someone enough to make that decision. That's why you'll typically see maybe around 1500 words for health supplements. Getting further along that scale, if you were to enroll someone in an advanced weight loss coaching program, that might take a full 50 minutes to really get them to the point where they're ready to make a decision on that. That would come in at around 5000 words. And as you get even more complex, maybe around a new investment opportunity, that might take you a good 90 minutes over a cup of coffee to be able to coach that conversion. And that's why a lot of the financial sales letters you'll see are massive and can come in at around 9000, 10000 words. So with all that said, I like to just sum this up with what I call the Starbucks test. If you can coach the conversion over an espresso shot, that's 100 and 500 words. If it would take you a small cappuccino to do it, you're looking at one to 3000 words. And if you need a full cup and a complimentary refill, you're looking at about 5000 words. So that's what we're doing when we're coaching the conversion on a sales page. And in the next lesson in module one, I'm going to go deeper into walking you through the 15 steps of the sales page and a day theme plate that really allow you to coach that conversion gradually step by step in a way that finally empowers your prospect to make that. I'll see you in the next lesson.
